data: advanced-concept-synthesis
Advanced Concept Synthesis
Advanced / Fundamentals
Synthesize advanced learning concepts across assessment, transfer, and learner judgment.
acs-1
multiple-choice
A team says its quiz measures 'critical thinking' because the questions are difficult. Which critique is strongest?
Difficulty alone does not establish that the assessment targets the intended construct
Any hard question automatically measures critical thinking
Constructs can never be measured with quiz questions
The word critical makes the construct self-evident
Ask whether the item evidence matches the intended ability.
The intended construct must be represented by the task demands, not just by difficulty. For further study, review construct validity and assessment alignment.
acs-2
Which design best supports far transfer after learners master a procedure in one context?
Vary surface features while preserving the underlying structure learners must recognize
Repeat one identical example until learners memorize its wording
Remove explanations and rely on speed only
Avoid asking learners to compare cases
Far transfer requires recognizing structure beyond familiar surface details.
Varying surface features helps learners abstract the underlying structure and apply it elsewhere. For further study, review transfer, analogical reasoning, and varied practice.
acs-3
true-false
Reliability is sufficient to prove that an assessment measures the right construct.
Consistency and correctness of interpretation are related but different.

Reliability concerns consistency; validity concerns whether score interpretations fit the intended use. A measure can be consistent and still miss the construct. For further study, review reliability versus validity.
acs-4
fill-blank
A learner's judgment about how well they know something is called _____.
metacognition
It is thinking about one's own thinking or learning.
Metacognition includes monitoring understanding and choosing strategies based on that judgment. For further study, review calibration and self-regulated learning.
acs-5
A learner performs well immediately after instruction but poorly one week later. Which interpretation is most precise?
Initial performance may not reflect durable retention
The learner never understood anything
The delayed quiz is automatically invalid
Immediate performance is always the only outcome that matters
Separate short-term performance from longer-term learning.

Immediate performance can be inflated by recent exposure or supports. Delayed retrieval gives different evidence about retention. For further study, review desirable difficulties and retention intervals.
acs-6
Which item best assesses schema formation rather than isolated recall?
Choose the principle that explains why three different examples work the same way
Repeat a definition exactly as written
Identify the first word in a paragraph
Select the answer choice with the shortest text
A schema organizes relationships across examples.
Identifying a shared principle across examples requires organized conceptual knowledge. For further study, review schema theory and case comparison.
acs-7
Interleaving can feel harder than blocked practice even when it improves later discrimination.
A strategy can reduce fluency during practice while improving later selection.
Interleaving forces learners to choose strategies across mixed examples, which can feel harder but improve later discrimination. For further study, review blocked versus interleaved practice.
acs-8
A question includes dense wording, unfamiliar notation, and two unrelated skills. What is the primary risk?
Extraneous cognitive load may obscure the skill being assessed
The item must be invalid because it has multiple words
All advanced items should be as dense as possible
Learners cannot handle any challenge
Challenge should come from the target skill, not avoidable clutter.
Extraneous load can make an item hard for reasons unrelated to the intended construct. For further study, review cognitive load theory and item-writing clarity.
acs-9
Which explanation best supports generative learning?
It asks learners to connect the answer to a principle and create a new example
It tells learners only whether they were right
It hides the reasoning so learners cannot copy it
It replaces practice with passive exposure
Generative learning requires learners to actively make meaning.
Connecting an answer to a principle and generating a new example encourages active construction of understanding. For further study, review self-explanation and generative learning.
acs-10
A learner's confidence matching their actual performance is called _____.
calibration
It compares judgment with reality.
Calibration describes how accurately confidence reflects actual performance. For further study, review metacognitive monitoring and confidence judgments.
acs-11
A quiz claims to measure analysis but rewards only speed. What design problem is most likely?
The scoring emphasis may privilege fluency over analytic quality
Speed is always the same as analysis
Analysis cannot be scored
The timer automatically improves validity
Scoring choices communicate what performance matters.
If scoring rewards speed more than reasoning, scores may reflect fluency rather than analytic quality. For further study, review scoring rubrics and construct representation.
acs-12
Which sequence best represents scaffolding for complex reasoning?
Model the reasoning, guide a partial attempt, then ask for independent justification
Start with the hardest task and remove all feedback
Give only final answers without process
Avoid practice until the final assessment
Support should fade as competence grows.
Scaffolding provides temporary support that is gradually removed as learners take more responsibility. For further study, review worked examples and fading guidance.
acs-13
A threshold concept can change how learners understand many later ideas in a domain.
The concept acts like a doorway into a new way of seeing the domain.
Threshold concepts are transformative ideas that reorganize understanding in a domain. For further study, review threshold concepts and conceptual change.
acs-14
A learner can solve examples but cannot explain why the method works. Which follow-up best probes deeper understanding?
Ask them to justify the method and identify when it would not apply
Ask them to repeat the exact same example faster
Remove all prompts and record no response
Only ask whether they liked the question
Deeper understanding includes reasons and boundaries.
Justifying the method and naming limits probes conceptual understanding beyond procedural success. For further study, review explanation prompts and transfer boundaries.
advanced-strategy-application
Advanced Strategy Application
Advanced / Application
Make expert-level instructional and problem-solving decisions under realistic constraints.
asa-1
A cohort has high completion but low delayed retention. Which intervention best targets the gap?
Add delayed retrieval and spaced review after the initial quiz
Make the quiz shorter but never revisit the content
Remove explanations to increase speed
Only celebrate completion and avoid measuring retention
The weak outcome is retention after time passes.
Delayed retrieval and spaced review directly target durable retention. For further study, review retrieval practice, spacing, and delayed post-tests.
asa-2
A learner misses scenario items because they choose the first familiar term they see. Which design change best helps?
Add prompts that require identifying the relevant constraint before answering
Add more familiar terms without context
Remove scenarios entirely
Make every answer choice a synonym
The issue is premature matching, not lack of vocabulary alone.
Identifying constraints slows pattern matching and focuses attention on what the scenario actually requires. For further study, review problem representation and expert-novice differences.
asa-3
Adaptive practice should use evidence from learner performance to decide what comes next.
Adaptive means responsive.
Adaptive practice changes difficulty, content, or support based on performance evidence. For further study, review mastery learning and adaptive sequencing.
asa-4
A team must reduce quiz length without losing coverage of a complex objective. Which approach is strongest?
Use fewer, richer scenarios that each require multiple targeted reasoning steps
Delete every difficult question first
Keep only questions that assess the same definition
Remove explanations and leave all items unchanged
Coverage can come from carefully designed tasks, not only item count.
Richer scenarios can preserve construct coverage while reducing item count. For further study, review assessment blueprints and evidence-centered design.
asa-5
A plan that maps objectives to question types and coverage is an assessment _____.
blueprint
It is an architectural plan for the assessment.
An assessment blueprint maps objectives, content, and item types to planned evidence. For further study, review test blueprints and table-of-specifications methods.
asa-6
A quiz is intended for learners using screen readers. Which content decision best supports accessibility?
Write prompts and explanations that do not depend on visual position alone
Use 'the green thing on the left' as the only clue
Put all meaning in color differences
Remove text labels from answer choices
Meaning should survive when visual layout is not available.
Accessible content should communicate meaning through text and structure, not only visual placement or color. For further study, review inclusive assessment design and WCAG text alternatives.
asa-7
A learner succeeds only when hints are visible. What is the best next step if the goal is independent performance?
Fade hints gradually while monitoring accuracy
Remove all practice opportunities
Keep hints permanent and stop measuring independence
Increase unrelated visual detail
The support should decrease without dropping the learner off a cliff.
Fading hints lets learners practice independence while preserving enough support to keep improving. For further study, review scaffolding and fading.
asa-8
When a quiz has a high-stakes purpose, item explanations are still useful for learning but may need to be separated from score reporting.
Think about learning feedback versus assessment security.
High-stakes contexts may require different timing or access rules for explanations, while still valuing feedback for later learning. For further study, review formative versus summative assessment.
asa-9
A team sees that learners miss questions with long prompts even when the concept is familiar. What should they investigate first?
Whether reading load or irrelevant detail is masking the target skill
Whether the concept should be deleted entirely
Whether all learners dislike quizzes
Whether shorter answer IDs would change learning
Long prompts can create demands unrelated to the target concept.
Prompt length and irrelevant detail may add extraneous load that interferes with performance. For further study, review cognitive load and item clarity.
asa-10
A course wants learners to choose among similar strategies. Which practice format best matches the goal?
Interleaved scenarios where learners must decide which strategy applies
Blocked drills that label the strategy in every prompt
Rereading the strategy list only
A single true/false question about strategy names
The goal includes selecting a strategy, not just executing a named one.
Interleaved scenarios require learners to discriminate among strategies, matching the decision skill. For further study, review interleaving and discrimination learning.
asa-11
A small check during learning that guides the next teaching move is a formative _____.
assessment
It is assessment used while learning is still in progress.
Formative assessment provides evidence that can shape instruction before final evaluation. For further study, review formative assessment cycles.
asa-12
A learner asks why an answer is wrong. Which feedback is most actionable?
Name the misconception, contrast it with the correct rule, and suggest a next practice step
Say only 'wrong' and move on
Reveal the score but hide the answer
Give a compliment unrelated to the response
Actionable feedback helps the learner change the next attempt.
Specific feedback that addresses the misconception and next step is more useful than a judgment alone. For further study, review feedback specificity and error correction.
asa-13
A scenario can assess multiple objectives, but the explanation should make clear which reasoning steps mattered.
Complex items need transparent reasoning after the answer.
When scenarios combine objectives, explanations should unpack the key reasoning steps to support learning. For further study, review complex item design and rationale writing.
asa-14
A team wants to personalize review without overwhelming learners. Which rule is most defensible?
Prioritize concepts that are important, recently missed, and prerequisites for later work
Always assign the longest possible review set
Only review topics the learner never misses
Randomize everything and ignore performance data
Personalization should consider importance, evidence, and sequence.
Important, recently missed prerequisite concepts are high-value targets for review. For further study, review adaptive practice policies and prerequisite mapping.
advanced-systems-analysis
Advanced Systems Analysis
Advanced / Analysis
Analyze messy evidence, competing explanations, and system-level learning outcomes.
asa2-1
Two sections use different quiz packs. Section A scores higher, but it also had more prior experience. What is the best interpretation?
Prior experience is a plausible confounder, so the pack difference is not isolated
The pack difference is proven as the only cause
Prior experience cannot affect quiz scores
The lower-scoring section must have ignored instructions
Look for a factor that differs between sections and could affect scores.
Prior experience may influence scores independently of the pack, so the causal interpretation is limited. For further study, review confounding and quasi-experimental comparisons.
asa2-2
A dashboard shows average score improved, but beginners improved while advanced learners declined. What issue does the average hide?
Different effects across learner subgroups
A missing quiz title
The impossibility of using averages
Proof that all learners improved
A single average can hide subgroup patterns.
Aggregate metrics can mask heterogeneous effects across learner groups. For further study, review subgroup analysis and Simpson's paradox.
asa2-3
Selection bias can occur when the learners who choose a quiz differ systematically from those who do not.
Voluntary participation can change who is represented.
If participants differ from nonparticipants, outcomes may reflect those differences as well as the quiz. For further study, review selection bias and sampling.
asa2-4
When a measure captures something different from the intended construct, the interpretation has weak _____.
validity
This word concerns whether the interpretation fits the evidence.
Validity concerns the degree to which evidence supports the intended interpretation and use of scores. For further study, review validity arguments.
asa2-5
Completion rate rises after hints are added, but average score falls. Which next question is most useful?
Did hints bring in more learners who attempted harder material or had lower starting knowledge?
Can completion and score never move in different directions?
Should all hints be removed without review?
Did the app stop counting completions?
A participation change can alter who is included in the score average.
Higher completion may include more or different learners, changing the average score population. For further study, review composition effects and metric interpretation.
asa2-6
A new pack is tested only with learners who already enjoy the topic. What external-validity concern appears?
Results may not generalize to less interested learners
The pack cannot be enjoyable for anyone
Interest makes all measures invalid
No learner characteristic can affect outcomes
Ask who the evidence represents.
Testing only with interested learners limits generalization to broader audiences. For further study, review external validity and sample representativeness.
asa2-7
A null result always means the tested strategy has no effect in any context.
A non-difference can have several explanations.
A null result may reflect no effect, low power, poor implementation, insensitive measures, or context limits. For further study, review statistical power and implementation fidelity.
asa2-8
Which evidence best supports a claim that a quiz improves transfer?
Learners perform better on new scenario types that require the same underlying principle
Learners remember the exact wording of one original question
Learners click through the quiz quickly
Learners prefer the quiz title
Transfer requires success beyond the original surface form.
Improvement on new scenarios with the same principle is direct evidence of transfer. For further study, review near and far transfer measures.
asa2-9
A metric tracks time spent but not whether learners are engaged with the task. What measurement risk is present?
Time may be an imperfect proxy for meaningful engagement
Time always proves deep engagement
Engagement cannot be measured in any way
The metric is perfect because it is numeric
A proxy is not the same as the construct.
Time spent can reflect engagement, confusion, distraction, or idle behavior, so it is an imperfect proxy. For further study, review proxy measures and construct validity.
asa2-10
The degree to which a result can apply beyond the original setting is external _____.
It is about generalizing outward.
External validity concerns whether findings generalize to other learners, settings, materials, or times. For further study, review generalizability and ecological validity.
asa2-11
A system improves average score by making the easiest packs more visible. What outcome should analysts check next?
Whether learning improved or learners shifted toward easier content
Whether the average score is now the only metric needed
Whether hard packs should be hidden permanently
Whether titles became shorter
Metric movement can come from behavior changes unrelated to learning.
Average score can rise if learners choose easier packs, even without deeper learning. For further study, review metric gaming and construct-aligned outcomes.
asa2-12
A study compares learners before and after a redesign, but another course requirement changed at the same time. What should be documented?
The concurrent change as a possible alternative explanation
Only the redesign, because other changes never matter
Nothing, because documentation weakens claims
The favorite color of each learner
Changes that happen together complicate causal claims.
Concurrent changes can explain outcomes, so they should be documented as alternative explanations. For further study, review threats to internal validity.
asa2-13
A metric can be useful for monitoring while still being insufficient for a causal conclusion.
Monitoring and causal inference require different strengths of evidence.
Operational metrics can reveal patterns worth watching, but causal conclusions require stronger designs. For further study, review descriptive analytics versus causal inference.
asa2-14
A pack shows high scores and low replay rates. Which interpretation is most balanced?
Learners may find it easy, complete it once, or not see value in replay; more evidence is needed
The pack is definitely perfect
The pack is definitely useless
Replay rate alone proves learning quality
Avoid overreading one pair of metrics.

High scores and low replay can have multiple explanations, so analysts should combine metrics with learner feedback or item-level evidence. For further study, review triangulation and mixed-method evaluation.
beginner-analysis
Beginner Analysis
Beginner / Analysis
Learn to spot simple patterns, compare evidence, and choose supported conclusions.
ban-1
A learner scores 4, 6, and 8 on three practice rounds. What simple pattern appears?
The score increases by 2 each round
The score decreases by 2 each round
The score stays the same
The score doubles each round
Compare each score to the one before it.
The scores rise from 4 to 6 to 8, so the increase is 2 points each time.
ban-2
A supported conclusion should match the evidence provided.
The conclusion should not go beyond what the evidence can show.
A conclusion is supported when it follows from the available evidence.
ban-3
A quiz explanation says, 'The plant grew taller after receiving more light.' Which relationship is being suggested?
More light may be connected to more growth
The plant stopped growing
Light never affects plants
The plant's height was not observed
Look for the answer that connects the change to the result.
The explanation suggests a possible relationship between receiving more light and growing taller.
ban-4
A statement that can be checked with evidence is a _____.
claim
It is what the evidence supports or challenges.
A claim is a checkable statement that can be supported or challenged with evidence.
ban-5
Which detail is most relevant for deciding whether a study plan worked?
Scores before and after using the plan
The color of the notebook
The first letter of the learner's name
The order of apps on the home screen
Relevant evidence should connect directly to the question.
Scores before and after the plan are directly related to whether performance changed.
ban-6
If two things happen at the same time, one must always have caused the other.
Timing alone is not the same as proof.
Events can happen together without one causing the other. More evidence is needed for a causal claim.
ban-7
Which answer shows the best first step in analysis?
Define the question you are trying to answer
Jump to the most dramatic conclusion
Ignore details that do not fit your first idea
Choose an answer before reading the evidence
Good analysis starts by knowing what problem you are solving.
Defining the question gives the analysis a clear target and helps decide what evidence matters.
ban-8
A possible answer that still needs evidence is a _____.
hypothesis
Scientists often test one.
A hypothesis is a possible explanation or answer that can be tested with evidence.
ban-9
Two sources disagree. What should you do before deciding which is stronger?
Check what evidence each source provides
Pick the source with the shorter title
Assume both sources are equally wrong
Choose the one you saw first
Source strength depends on support, not order.
Comparing the evidence behind each source is a basic way to judge which claim is better supported.
ban-10
Analysis can include deciding which details are relevant and which are background.
Analysis often starts by sorting information.
Separating relevant details from background information helps focus the reasoning.
beginner-application
Beginner Application
Beginner / Application
Practice simple study and reasoning moves in everyday learning scenarios.
ba-1
You miss a question about vocabulary. What is the best next step?
Read the explanation and make a simple example using the word
Skip the word forever
Assume every future vocabulary question will be wrong
Memorize only the answer letter
Choose the action that helps you use the concept again.
Reading the explanation and making an example turns feedback into active practice.
ba-2
When applying a new concept, using a simple example can make the idea easier to check.
Simple examples reduce the number of moving parts.
A simple example makes it easier to see whether the concept is being used correctly.
ba-3
A learner wants to remember three key terms. Which plan is strongest?
Review the terms today, tomorrow, and later in the week
Look at the terms once and close the notes
Only review the term that is already easiest
Wait until the quiz starts to read them
Think about spaced practice.
Reviewing over time uses spaced practice, which supports longer-term memory.
ba-4
Before choosing an answer, it helps to restate the question in your own _____.
words
The phrase is often 'in your own ...'
Restating a question in your own words checks that you understand what is being asked.
ba-5
You are comparing two answer choices. What should you do first?
Identify the key difference between the choices
Pick the longer answer automatically
Ignore the prompt
Choose the first answer listed
Comparison starts by noticing what is not the same.
Identifying the key difference helps connect the answer choices back to the question.
ba-6
If a scenario includes extra details, every detail must be equally important.
Some details are background information.
Scenarios often include context. The learner should separate relevant details from background details.
ba-7
A classmate says, 'This worked once, so it always works.' Which response applies better reasoning?
Ask for more examples before treating it as a rule
Agree that one example proves every case
Ignore all examples
Change the subject to avoid evidence
One example can be useful, but it may not prove a universal rule.
Asking for more examples checks whether the pattern holds beyond a single case.
ba-8
A useful answer explanation should name the correct answer and explain the _____.
reason
It tells why the answer works.
The reason teaches the concept and helps the learner handle similar questions later.
ba-9
You have ten minutes to review. Which task is most active?
Answer practice questions without looking first
Highlight every sentence in the notes
Scroll through the page without pausing
Move the notes into a new folder
Active review asks you to retrieve or use information.
Practice questions require retrieval, which is more active than simply rereading or organizing.
ba-10
Checking why the wrong choices are wrong can improve future performance.
Wrong choices can reveal the boundaries of a concept.
Reviewing distractors helps learners understand common misconceptions and avoid repeating them.
beginner-fundamentals
Beginner Fundamentals
Beginner / Fundamentals
Build confidence with essential learning, evidence, and reasoning concepts.
bf-1
Which phrase best describes a learning objective?
A measurable skill or understanding the learner should gain
A random fact placed at the end of a quiz
The color used for correct answers
A list of every possible topic in a subject
Look for the option that describes what the learner can do afterward.
A learning objective names the outcome learners should be able to demonstrate.
bf-2
Feedback is most useful when it explains why an answer is correct or incorrect.
Think about what helps someone improve on the next attempt.
Feedback should teach the reason behind the answer so the learner can transfer the idea.
bf-3
A repeated arrangement or trend is called a _____.
pattern
It is what you notice when the same kind of thing happens again and again.
A pattern is a repeated arrangement, behavior, or relationship that can help guide predictions.
bf-4
Which item is evidence?
A measurement recorded during an experiment
A guess made before looking at anything
A preference for one answer style
A topic someone wants to study later
Evidence is something you can point to in support of a claim.
Evidence is information that supports or challenges a claim, such as a measurement, observation, or source.
bf-5
What is the best definition of a claim?
A statement that can be supported or challenged
A timer used during a quiz
A list of answer choices
A score shown after a round
A claim says something that evidence can test.
A claim is a statement that can be evaluated with reasoning and evidence.
bf-6
A summary should include every tiny detail from the original material.
A good summary is shorter than the original.
A summary captures the main ideas and leaves out details that are not essential.
bf-7
Which practice best supports long-term memory?
Spacing practice across multiple sessions
Reading once as quickly as possible
Avoiding review after the first attempt
Only studying material that already feels easy
Memory usually improves when review is spread out.
Spaced practice gives the brain repeated chances to retrieve and strengthen the material.
bf-8
Information around a fact or event that helps explain its meaning is called _____.
context
It is the surrounding situation.
Context is the surrounding information that helps a fact, quote, or event make sense.
bf-9
Which question asks about cause and effect?
What happened after the rule changed?
What color is the chart title?
How many choices are on the screen?
Which word appears first alphabetically?
Cause and effect links an action or condition to a result.
Asking what happened after a change looks for a relationship between a cause and an effect.
bf-10
A conclusion should connect back to the evidence used in the explanation.
A conclusion is strongest when the reader can see where it came from.
A conclusion is more convincing when it follows from the evidence and reasoning already presented.
nyt-easy
Easy Mini-Style Trivia
Word play
Short, breezy questions in the spirit of a daily mini puzzle. Built for warming up.
ne-1
Tropical orange-fleshed stone fruit, five letters:
Peach
Mango
Guava
Melon
Mango — five letters, fits the clue.
ne-2
Largest planet in our solar system:
Saturn
Neptune
Earth
Jupiter
Jupiter is the largest, with a mass greater than all other planets combined.
ne-3
Capital of France:
Lyon
Marseille
Paris
Nice
Paris.
ne-4
Number of sides on a hexagon:
Five
Six
Seven
Eight
Hex- = six. Octagon has eight, pentagon has five.
ne-5
Which of these is a primary color?
Green
Orange
Yellow
Purple
The three primary colors of light are red, green, and blue; in paint they are red, yellow, and blue. Yellow is the only primary in this list.
ne-6
Author of 'Romeo and Juliet':
Charles Dickens
Jane Austen
Mark Twain
William Shakespeare
Shakespeare wrote it around 1595.
ne-7
Smallest U.S. state by area:
Delaware
Connecticut
Rhode Island
Hawaii
Rhode Island is about 1,545 square miles.
ne-8
Currency used in Japan:
Won
Yuan
Yen
Ringgit
The yen has been Japan's currency since 1871.
ne-9
Tallest mountain on Earth, measured from sea level:
K2
Mount Everest
Kangchenjunga
Denali
Everest stands at 8,849 metres.
ne-10
How many continents are there?
Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australia, Europe, North America, and South America.
intermediate-concept-connections
Intermediate Concept Connections
Intermediate / Fundamentals
Connect learning, reasoning, and evidence concepts across slightly deeper examples.
icc-1
A learner rereads a chapter twice but still cannot answer practice questions. Which concept best explains the gap?
Recognition can feel familiar without proving retrieval
Rereading always removes the need for practice
Practice questions only measure typing speed
Familiarity and mastery are identical
Think about the difference between seeing an answer and producing it.
Rereading can create familiarity, but retrieval practice checks whether the learner can recall and use the idea.
icc-2
The amount of mental effort a task places on working memory is cognitive _____.
load
The phrase describes how heavy the task feels mentally.
Cognitive load is the demand a task places on working memory.
icc-3
If two measures move together, that pattern alone proves that one caused the other.
A relationship can exist without proving direction or cause.
Correlation can suggest a relationship, but causal claims need stronger evidence and attention to alternative explanations.
icc-4
Why can comparing an example and a nonexample help someone learn a concept?
It highlights the defining feature that makes the concept apply
It guarantees every future problem has only one detail
It removes the need for explanations
It makes the concept depend on answer order
A nonexample is useful because it almost fits, but not quite.
Example and nonexample comparisons clarify the boundaries of a concept by showing what counts and what does not.
icc-5
A quiz objective says learners should 'compare two explanations,' but every question asks for definitions only. What is misaligned?
The assessment does not match the objective
The objective has too many words
Definitions can never be assessed
The quiz title is too short
Compare the skill named by the objective with the skill required by the questions.
Assessment questions should give learners a chance to demonstrate the skill named in the objective.
icc-6
Which schedule best combines spacing and retrieval?
Short practice attempts on three different days with answers hidden first
One long rereading session with all answers visible
A single review after the quiz ends
Only reviewing the easiest card repeatedly
The best option spreads practice and asks the learner to recall.
Short attempts across multiple days with hidden answers combine spaced practice and retrieval practice.
icc-7
A cycle where an attempt leads to information that improves the next attempt is a feedback _____.
loop
The process circles back into the next try.
A feedback loop uses results from one attempt to adjust the next attempt.
icc-8
A student sorts four scenarios by whether they include measurable evidence. Which thinking skill are they practicing?
Classification using a defining criterion
Memorizing answer positions
Avoiding comparison
Changing the topic
They are grouping examples according to a rule.
Classification applies a criterion to decide which items belong together.
icc-9
Transfer means using a concept in a new situation, not only repeating it in the original example.
The key idea is using learning somewhere else.
Transfer happens when learners apply knowledge or skills beyond the exact practice context.
icc-10
A lesson starts with a worked example, then asks learners to solve a similar problem with fewer hints. Why is this sequence useful?
It gradually shifts responsibility from the example to the learner
It prevents learners from ever practicing independently
It makes the explanation optional in every case
It hides the goal of the lesson
The support decreases as the learner gains footing.
Fading support helps learners move from guided understanding toward independent performance.
icc-11
Knowledge needed before learning a more complex idea is called _____ knowledge.
prerequisite
It comes before the new material.
Prerequisite knowledge is the foundation a learner needs before tackling a more advanced concept.
icc-12
A concept map is most useful when a learner needs to show what?
Relationships among ideas
The fastest way to click a button
A single memorized definition only
The number of letters in each answer
Concept maps connect nodes.
Concept maps help learners organize and explain relationships among ideas.
intermediate-evidence-review
Intermediate Evidence Review
Intermediate / Analysis
Evaluate claims, spot limits in evidence, and choose careful conclusions.
ier-1
A class tried a new review method. Average scores rose from 70 to 78. What is the most careful conclusion?
Scores improved after the method, but more evidence is needed to prove the method caused it
The method caused every student to improve
The method had no possible relationship to the scores
The scores prove the next class will average 90
Before-and-after evidence is useful but limited.
The scores improved, but without more controls or context, causation should be stated carefully.
ier-2
A survey includes only three people from a group of two hundred. What is the main limitation?
The sample may be too small to represent the full group
The survey has too many participants
The results must be perfectly accurate
The group size no longer matters
Ask whether the evidence represents the larger group.
A very small sample can miss important variation in the full group.
ier-3
When two sources disagree, the stronger source is always the one that sounds more confident.
Confidence and evidence are different.
Source strength depends on evidence, method, relevance, and reliability, not just confident wording.
ier-4
A hidden factor that may affect both the supposed cause and the result is a _____.
confounder
It can confuse the relationship being studied.
A confounder is an outside factor that can distort a cause-and-effect interpretation.
ier-5
A learner's practice scores are 7, 8, 9, 3, and 10. What should an analyst notice first?
There is a generally rising pattern with one unusually low score
The scores are all identical
The final score is the lowest
There is no information to compare
Look for both the trend and the unusual value.
The scores mostly rise, while the 3 stands out as a possible outlier or special case.
ier-6
A claim says, 'This strategy works for everyone,' but the evidence comes from one learner. What is the issue?
The claim is broader than the evidence supports
The evidence is too broad for the claim
One learner proves all possible cases
The claim is automatically false because it is short
Compare the size of the claim with the size of the evidence.
Evidence from one learner can be useful, but it cannot justify a universal claim by itself.
ier-7
Which evidence would best test whether spaced practice helped a class?
Scores from comparable learners using spaced and non-spaced practice
The color of the classroom walls
A single comment from someone who did not practice
The number of pages in the textbook only
A comparison helps isolate the practice method.
Comparable groups using different practice schedules would give stronger evidence about the effect of spacing.
ier-8
An outlier should be deleted automatically before anyone asks why it happened.
Unusual data can be a mistake, but it can also be meaningful.
Outliers should be investigated before deciding whether they are errors or important signals.
ier-9
A chart shows completion rate but not score quality. What question remains unanswered?
Whether learners completed the quiz accurately
Whether anyone opened the quiz
Whether the quiz has a title
Whether the chart uses numbers
Completion and quality are related but not identical.
A completion rate shows who finished, but it does not show whether their answers were correct.
ier-10
A reasonable explanation that could also fit the evidence is an alternative _____.
explanation
It is another way to explain the same evidence.
Considering alternative explanations helps avoid jumping to a single unsupported cause.
ier-11
Which wording is most appropriate when evidence is suggestive but not conclusive?
The evidence suggests a possible relationship
The evidence proves the relationship in every case
No relationship can exist
The question cannot be discussed
Careful wording should match the strength of the evidence.
Suggestive evidence supports cautious language, not absolute proof.
ier-12
A learner improves after getting tutoring and also begins sleeping more. What is the best analysis move?
Consider that tutoring, sleep, or both may have contributed
Assume tutoring is the only possible cause
Assume sleep is impossible to consider
Ignore both changes
More than one change happened at the same time.
When multiple factors change, analysis should consider more than one possible contributor.
intermediate-practical-decisions
Intermediate Practical Decisions
Intermediate / Application
Choose effective study, feedback, and problem-solving moves in realistic situations.
ipd-1
A diagnostic quiz shows a learner misses main-idea questions but gets vocabulary correct. What should the next practice set emphasize?
Main-idea questions with explanations for how to identify central points
Only the vocabulary cards the learner already knows
A random set with no review afterward
More questions about the quiz interface
Use the diagnostic result to target the weakest skill.
Practice should respond to the evidence. The missed main-idea questions reveal the best target.
ipd-2
A prompt asks for the 'best supported conclusion.' Which action should come before choosing an answer?
Identify the evidence and eliminate conclusions that go beyond it
Choose the answer with the strongest wording
Ignore the evidence and pick the familiar topic
Select the answer with the most commas
Supported conclusions must stay close to the evidence.
The learner should compare each conclusion with the evidence and reject claims that overreach.
ipd-3
If a learner keeps making the same error, the next explanation should address the misconception directly.
Repeated errors are clues about the learner's current model.
Directly addressing the misconception helps the learner revise the mistaken reasoning.
ipd-4
A team has three days to prepare for a quiz. Which plan best uses the time?
Day 1: diagnose gaps; Day 2: practice weak areas; Day 3: mixed review
Day 1: do nothing; Day 2: do nothing; Day 3: read everything once
Spend all three days only formatting notes
Practice only the questions everyone already gets correct
A strong plan changes as evidence comes in.
Diagnose, target weak areas, then mix review creates a useful progression.
ipd-5
When a learner uses feedback to change the next attempt, they are making an _____.
adjustment
The next attempt is modified.
An adjustment is a change made in response to feedback or new evidence.
ipd-6
A scenario includes five details, but only two affect the answer. What is the best strategy?
Mark which details change the decision and which provide background
Treat every detail as equally decisive
Ignore the prompt and focus on the longest sentence
Choose the answer that mentions all five details
Application often requires sorting relevant from irrelevant information.
Separating decision-changing details from background helps keep the answer aligned with the scenario.
ipd-7
A good practice sequence can start easy and become more mixed as the learner improves.
Think about building confidence before transfer.
Starting with focused practice and moving toward mixed practice supports both accuracy and transfer.
ipd-8
A learner is stuck between two answers. One matches the evidence exactly; the other adds an unsupported prediction. Which should they choose?
The answer that matches the evidence exactly
The answer with the unsupported prediction
Whichever answer appears later
The answer with more technical words
Unsupported additions are risky.
The best supported answer should not add claims that the evidence does not justify.
ipd-9
Using a skill in a different but related situation is called _____.
transfer
It means the learning moves to a new context.
Transfer is applying learning beyond the original practice example.
ipd-10
A facilitator wants quieter participants to reveal what they understand. Which approach best supports that goal?
Use a short individual written response before group discussion
Let only the fastest speaker answer every question
Skip all checks for understanding
Ask for a final grade before practice begins
Give everyone a chance to think before speaking.
Individual written responses can surface understanding from people who may not speak first in a group.
ipd-11
When applying a rule, checking a borderline example can reveal whether you understand the rule's limits.
Borderline cases test the edges.
Borderline examples show where a rule applies and where it stops applying.
ipd-12
A learner has mastered definitions but struggles with scenarios. Which new question type should be added?
Short scenarios that ask the learner to choose the best action
More direct definition recall only
Questions with no explanations
Answer choices unrelated to the prompt
Practice should resemble the skill that needs improvement.
Scenario questions give the learner practice applying definitions in context.
intermediate-progressive-challenge
Intermediate Progressive Challenge
Intermediate / Mixed
A mixed review that ramps from core concepts to scenario analysis.
ipc-1
Which pair best matches a concept with its purpose?
Learning objective: defines the intended learner outcome
Distractor: proves the correct answer
Confounder: removes all uncertainty
Feedback: hides the reason for the answer
Look for the pair where the definition truly fits the term.
A learning objective states what the learner should know or be able to do.
ipc-2
A good distractor should be plausible enough to reveal a misconception.
Distractors are not random filler.
Plausible distractors help diagnose misunderstandings and make explanations more useful.
ipc-3
Applying a known idea to a new situation is called _____.
The idea moves from one context to another.
Transfer means using learning in a different but related situation.
ipc-4
A learner answers definitions correctly but misses scenario questions. What is the most likely next need?
Practice applying the definitions in context
More practice copying definitions only
A lower-quality explanation
A quiz with no feedback
The gap is not basic recall; it is use in context.
Scenario misses suggest the learner needs application practice, not only more definition recall.
ipc-5
A result says, '8 of 10 learners finished the quiz.' What does this directly measure?
Completion rate
Average score quality
Question difficulty
Cause of improvement
Finishing and scoring are different outcomes.
The statement directly reports completion, not accuracy or causation.
ipc-6
A single score can be useful evidence, but it rarely tells the whole story by itself.
One data point may need context.
A single score can guide a next step, but trends, comparisons, and context make the evidence stronger.
ipc-7
A question asks learners to pick the strongest claim from a set of evidence. Which answer pattern is safest?
The claim that is specific and directly supported
The claim that sounds absolute
The claim that ignores the evidence
The claim that adds the most new assumptions
Strong claims are not always broad claims.
A specific, directly supported claim is stronger than a broad claim that overreaches.
ipc-8
A detail that changes which answer is best is _____.
relevant
It matters to the decision.
Relevant details affect the answer or reasoning path.
ipc-9
A teacher wants to know whether students can explain, not just select, the answer. Which item is best?
A short-answer prompt asking for the reason behind a choice
A multiple-choice item with no explanation afterward
A timer-only activity
A question that asks for the student's favorite color
The item should require the learner to produce reasoning.
A short-answer prompt can reveal whether the learner can explain the reason, not only recognize an answer.
ipc-10
A learner improves on practice questions but not on mixed review. What is a likely issue?
They may recognize isolated patterns but struggle to choose strategies when topics are mixed
They have already mastered every possible skill
Mixed review cannot reveal anything useful
Practice questions caused the app to lose data
Mixed review tests selection as well as knowledge.
Mixed review requires learners to decide which concept or strategy applies, which is harder than focused practice.
ipc-11
When a scenario has multiple plausible answers, the best answer should be the one most supported by the scenario constraints.
Constraints narrow the answer choices.
Scenario constraints help determine which plausible answer is strongest in that specific case.
ipc-12
A learner must decide what to study after missing three question types: definitions, scenarios, and evidence evaluation. Which plan is most strategic?
Start with definitions, apply them in scenarios, then review evidence evaluation with explanations
Only study the last missed question
Avoid the missed topics and replay mastered questions
Randomly pick one topic and ignore feedback
Build from foundations toward more complex analysis.
The plan progresses from fundamentals to application to analysis, matching the increasing complexity of the missed skills.
premier-league
Premier League Trivia
Sports
From the 1992 inaugural season to today — how well do you know the EPL?
pl-1
Who is the Premier League's all-time top scorer?
Wayne Rooney
Alan Shearer
Harry Kane
Sergio Agüero
Alan Shearer scored 260 Premier League goals, mostly with Newcastle United.
pl-2
Which club won the inaugural Premier League title in the 1992–93 season?
Arsenal
Liverpool
Manchester United
Leeds United
Manchester United, managed by Sir Alex Ferguson, won the first ever Premier League.
pl-3
Erling Haaland joined Manchester City in 2022 from which club?
RB Leipzig
Bayer Leverkusen
Red Bull Salzburg
Borussia Dortmund
Haaland transferred from Borussia Dortmund and won the Golden Boot in his first PL season.
pl-4
Which stadium is home to Liverpool FC?
Anfield
Goodison Park
Old Trafford
Stamford Bridge
Anfield has been Liverpool's home since 1892.
pl-5
Who has won the most Premier League Golden Boots?
Thierry Henry
Both Henry and Shearer are tied
Thierry Henry and Alan Shearer are tied on four Golden Boots each.
pl-6
Mohamed Salah's national team is:
Morocco
Algeria
Egypt
Tunisia
Salah captains the Egyptian national team.
pl-7
Which club is nicknamed 'The Seagulls'?
Bournemouth
Brighton & Hove Albion
Crystal Palace
Southampton
Brighton & Hove Albion, named for their coastal home.
pl-8
Which player holds the record for most Premier League appearances?
James Milner
Gareth Barry
Ryan Giggs
Frank Lampard
Gareth Barry made 653 PL appearances across Aston Villa, Manchester City, Everton, and West Brom.
pl-9
Which manager led Leicester City to their fairytale 2015–16 title?
Brendan Rodgers
Claudio Ranieri
Nigel Pearson
Roberto Martínez
Claudio Ranieri's Leicester won the title at 5000-1 preseason odds.
pl-10
What is the largest stadium currently used in the Premier League by capacity?
Etihad Stadium
Tottenham Hotspur Stadium
London Stadium
Old Trafford holds approximately 74,000.
pl-11
Which club won four Premier League titles in a row from 2020–21 to 2023–24?
Chelsea
Manchester City
Manchester City's four consecutive titles is a PL record.
pl-12
Newcastle United play their home games at:
St James' Park
Stadium of Light
St Mary's
St James' Park has been Newcastle's home since 1892.
pl-13
Who is Arsenal's all-time leading goal scorer in the Premier League era?
Robin van Persie
Ian Wright
Dennis Bergkamp
Thierry Henry is Arsenal's all-time top scorer with 228 goals across all competitions.
pl-14
In what year was the Premier League founded?
The Premier League was founded in February 1992.
pl-15
Which goalkeeper has the most Premier League clean sheets all-time?
David Seaman
Petr Čech
Edwin van der Sar
David de Gea
Petr Čech finished his career with 202 PL clean sheets, a league record.
